tagline: Calculate the optimal sealed or ported speaker enclosure volume for any driver
keywords: speaker box volume, enclosure calculator, subwoofer box, sealed box, ported box

faq.question: What size box do I need for a 12-inch subwoofer?
faq.answer: A sealed 12" sub typically needs 1.0-1.75 cubic feet. A ported 12" sub needs 1.5-3.0 cubic feet. Always check the manufacturer's Thiele-Small parameters for best results.

faq.question: Should I build a sealed or ported box?
faq.answer: Sealed boxes give tighter, more accurate bass and are simpler to build. Ported boxes are louder (about +3 dB) and go deeper but are larger and less forgiving of design errors.

faq.question: What are Thiele-Small parameters?
faq.answer: Thiele-Small (T/S) parameters describe a speaker driver's electromechanical properties. The key ones for box design are Vas (compliance volume), Qts (total Q factor), and Fs (resonant frequency).

tagline: Convert between decibels, watts, and voltages — add or compare sound levels
keywords: decibel calculator, dB converter, sound level, power to dB, voltage to dB

faq.question: How do you add decibels together?
faq.answer: You can't simply add dB values. Two sources at 80 dB don't make 160 dB — they make 83 dB. Use: combined = 10 × log₁₀(10^(dB1/10) + 10^(dB2/10)).

faq.question: How much louder is 10 dB?
faq.answer: A 10 dB increase is perceived as roughly twice as loud. A 3 dB increase doubles the acoustic power. A 1 dB change is the smallest difference most people can detect.

faq.question: How does distance affect sound level?
faq.answer: Sound drops 6 dB each time distance doubles (inverse square law in free field). A speaker at 90 dB/1m will be about 84 dB at 2m and 78 dB at 4m.

tagline: Calculate the reverberation time of a room based on volume and surface absorption
keywords: reverb time, RT60 calculator, room acoustics, Sabine equation, reverberation

faq.question: What is RT60?
faq.answer: RT60 is the time it takes for sound to decay by 60 dB (become inaudible) after the source stops. A large cathedral might have RT60 of 5+ seconds; a recording studio aims for 0.3-0.5 seconds.

faq.question: What RT60 is ideal for a home studio?
faq.answer: For music recording: 0.3-0.5 seconds. For speech/podcasting: 0.2-0.4 seconds. For mixing/mastering: 0.3-0.6 seconds. Living rooms typically measure 0.4-0.8 seconds.

faq.question: How do I reduce reverberation?
faq.answer: Add absorption: acoustic panels, thick curtains, carpet, upholstered furniture, bookshelves. Each material has a specific absorption coefficient at different frequencies. Treat first reflection points first.

formulas: A = Total Absorption (sabins) = Surface Area × Average Absorption Coefficient

tagline: Calculate maximum cable run lengths and signal loss for audio installations
keywords: audio cable length, signal loss, cable run, balanced cable, speaker wire gauge

faq.question: How long can a balanced audio cable be?
faq.answer: Balanced cables (XLR, TRS) can run up to 1000 feet with minimal signal loss thanks to common-mode noise rejection. Most professional installations use runs up to 300 feet without issues.

faq.question: What gauge speaker wire do I need?
faq.answer: For runs under 50 feet: 16 AWG is fine for most speakers. 50-100 feet: use 14 AWG. Over 100 feet: use 12 AWG. Higher-powered systems and lower-impedance speakers need thicker wire.

faq.question: Why are unbalanced cables limited to 25 feet?
faq.answer: Unbalanced cables (TS, RCA) pick up electromagnetic interference that increases with length. Beyond 25 feet, noise becomes noticeable. Use balanced cables (XLR) for longer runs.

formulas: Wire Resistance = Resistance per Foot × Length × 2 (round trip)
Speaker Power Loss % = Wire Resistance ÷ (Wire Resistance + Speaker Impedance) × 100
Damping Factor = Speaker Impedance ÷ Wire Resistance

tagline: Calculate crossover filter component values for passive speaker crossovers
keywords: crossover frequency, speaker crossover, low pass filter, high pass filter, crossover calculator

faq.question: What crossover frequency should I use?
faq.answer: Common choices: Sub/mid crossover at 80-200 Hz. Mid/tweeter crossover at 2,000-4,000 Hz. Always cross over above the tweeter's resonant frequency (usually 800-1500 Hz).

faq.question: What order crossover is best?
faq.answer: 1st order: simplest, best phase response, gentle slope. 2nd order (Butterworth): most common, good balance. 3rd order: steeper rolloff protects tweeters, but more components and phase shift.

faq.question: Do I need a passive or active crossover?
faq.answer: Passive crossovers are simpler (no power needed) and go between the amp and speakers. Active crossovers go before the amps, giving better control but requiring multiple amplifiers.

formulas: High-pass is the inverse: swap C and L roles
Higher orders add additional filter stages

tagline: Convert BPM to delay times, note values, and frequency for music production
keywords: bpm to ms, delay time calculator, tempo calculator, note duration, music production

faq.question: Why convert BPM to milliseconds?
faq.answer: Delay effects, reverb pre-delay, and LFO rates sound best when synced to the song tempo. Setting a delay to the exact ms value of a note division keeps it rhythmically in time.

faq.question: What is a dotted note?
faq.answer: A dotted note is 1.5× the duration of the regular note. A dotted eighth note at 120 BPM = 375ms (vs 250ms for a straight eighth). Dotted delays create a bouncing rhythmic feel.

faq.question: What is a triplet delay?
faq.answer: A triplet divides a note into 3 equal parts instead of 2. A triplet eighth note at 120 BPM = 167ms (vs 250ms for straight). Triplet delays create a shuffle or swing feel.

formulas: Other notes = Quarter × Note Value × Modifier

tagline: Calculate audio file sizes for WAV, FLAC, MP3, and other formats
keywords: audio file size, wav size, mp3 size, audio bitrate, recording size

faq.question: How big is a 3-minute WAV file?
faq.answer: A 3-minute stereo 44.1kHz/16-bit WAV file is about 30 MB. At 48kHz/24-bit (studio quality), it's about 50 MB. Higher sample rates and bit depths increase size proportionally.

faq.question: How much smaller is FLAC than WAV?
faq.answer: FLAC typically compresses to 50-60% of the WAV size with zero quality loss. A 30 MB WAV becomes about 16-18 MB FLAC. Compression varies with audio content.

faq.question: What sample rate and bit depth should I record at?
faq.answer: For most recording: 48kHz/24-bit is the professional standard. 44.1kHz/16-bit is CD quality. 96kHz is useful for recordings that will be heavily processed or pitch-shifted.

formulas: WAV Size = Sample Rate × (Bit Depth ÷ 8) × Channels × Duration
Uncompressed Bitrate = Sample Rate × Bit Depth × Channels

tagline: Calculate your cryptocurrency profit or loss including fees
keywords: crypto profit, crypto gain loss, bitcoin profit, crypto ROI, investment return

faq.question: How do I calculate crypto profit?
faq.answer: Profit = (Sell Price × Coins Owned × (1 - Sell Fee)) - Original Investment. Always account for exchange fees on both buying and selling, which typically range from 0.1% to 1.5%.

faq.question: Do I have to pay taxes on crypto profits?
faq.answer: In most countries, yes. Crypto gains are typically taxed as capital gains. Short-term gains (held less than 1 year) are taxed at higher rates than long-term gains in the US.

faq.question: What is ROI in crypto?
faq.answer: Return on Investment (ROI) = (Profit ÷ Investment) × 100%. An ROI of 50% means you earned $500 on a $1,000 investment. Crypto ROI can be highly volatile.

formulas: Coins Owned = (Investment × (1 - Buy Fee%)) ÷ Buy Price
Current Value = Coins × Sell Price × (1 - Sell Fee%)

tagline: Calculate if your crypto mining setup is profitable after electricity and hardware costs
keywords: mining profitability, crypto mining calculator, bitcoin mining, hashrate profit

faq.question: Is crypto mining still profitable?
faq.answer: It depends on electricity costs, hardware efficiency, and coin price. At $0.05/kWh or less, mining can be profitable. Above $0.10/kWh, only the most efficient machines profit. Always run the numbers.

faq.question: What affects mining profitability most?
faq.answer: Electricity cost is the biggest factor, followed by hardware efficiency (hashrate per watt), network difficulty, and coin price. A 50% drop in electricity cost can turn a loss into profit.

faq.question: How long does it take for a mining rig to pay for itself?
faq.answer: Typical breakeven is 12-24 months under favorable conditions. If breakeven exceeds 18 months, the risk increases as difficulty adjustments and price changes could eliminate profits.

formulas: Daily Revenue = Hashrate × Coins per TH/day × Coin Price
Daily Electricity = (Watts ÷ 1000) × 24 hours × Rate per kWh

tagline: Calculate DeFi staking and liquidity pool returns with compounding
keywords: defi yield, APY calculator, staking returns, liquidity pool, yield farming

faq.question: What is APY vs APR in DeFi?
faq.answer: APR is the simple annual rate without compounding. APY includes the effect of compounding. A 12% APR compounded daily gives about 12.75% APY. Always compare APY to APY.

faq.question: What is impermanent loss?
faq.answer: Impermanent loss occurs in liquidity pools when one token's price changes relative to the other. A 50% price change in one token causes about 5.7% impermanent loss. It's 'impermanent' because it reverses if prices return to original levels.

faq.question: Are high DeFi yields sustainable?
faq.answer: Extremely high APYs (100%+) are usually temporary and decrease as more capital enters the pool. Sustainable yields are typically 3-15% for stablecoins and 10-30% for volatile pairs.

tagline: Estimate capital gains tax on cryptocurrency profits (US tax rates)
keywords: crypto tax, capital gains tax, bitcoin tax, crypto tax rate, tax calculator

faq.question: How is cryptocurrency taxed in the US?
faq.answer: The IRS treats crypto as property. Selling, trading, or spending crypto triggers capital gains tax. Short-term gains (held < 1 year) are taxed as ordinary income. Long-term gains get preferential rates of 0%, 15%, or 20%.

faq.question: Do I owe tax on crypto I haven't sold?
faq.answer: No. Unrealized gains (crypto you still hold) are not taxable. Tax is only triggered when you sell, trade crypto-to-crypto, or spend crypto on goods/services.

faq.question: Can I offset crypto losses against gains?
faq.answer: Yes. Crypto losses can offset crypto gains dollar-for-dollar. Excess losses can offset up to $3,000 of ordinary income per year, with remaining losses carried forward.

formulas: Capital Gain = Sale Price - Purchase Price
Short-term Tax = Gain × Ordinary Income Tax Rate
Long-term Tax = Gain × (0%, 15%, or 20% depending on bracket)
Net Profit = Capital Gain - Tax Owed

tagline: See how dollar-cost averaging performs vs lump-sum investing in volatile assets
keywords: dollar cost averaging, DCA calculator, recurring investment, crypto DCA

faq.question: What is dollar-cost averaging (DCA)?
faq.answer: DCA means investing a fixed amount at regular intervals regardless of price. You buy more when prices are low and less when high, which can lower your average purchase price in volatile markets.

faq.question: Is DCA better than lump-sum investing?
faq.answer: Historically, lump-sum beats DCA about 2/3 of the time in rising markets. However, DCA reduces risk of bad timing and is psychologically easier. In volatile or declining markets, DCA often wins.

faq.question: How often should I DCA?
faq.answer: Weekly or monthly are most common. More frequent DCA (weekly) provides slightly better averaging but the difference is small. Choose whatever schedule you can stick to consistently.

formulas: Total Invested = Monthly Amount × Number of Months
Average Price = Total Invested ÷ Total Coins Purchased
DCA Value = Total Coins × End Price
DCA Return = (Final Value - Total Invested) ÷ Total Invested × 100%

tagline: Estimate Ethereum gas fees for transactions, swaps, and NFT mints
keywords: gas fee, ethereum gas, gwei calculator, transaction fee, ETH gas cost

faq.question: What are Ethereum gas fees?
faq.answer: Gas fees are payments to validators for processing transactions on Ethereum. Fees = Gas Used × Gas Price (in Gwei). 1 Gwei = 0.000000001 ETH. Fees vary based on network congestion.

faq.question: Why are gas fees so high sometimes?
faq.answer: Gas fees spike during high network demand — NFT drops, DeFi events, or market volatility. When many users compete for block space, gas prices can exceed 200+ Gwei vs the typical 10-30 Gwei.

faq.question: How can I reduce gas fees?
faq.answer: Transact during off-peak hours (weekends, early AM UTC). Use Layer 2 solutions (Arbitrum, Optimism, Base) for 10-100× cheaper fees. Batch transactions when possible.

formulas: Gas Fee = (Base Fee + Priority Fee) × Gas Used
Cost in USD = ETH Cost × ETH Price
